exercises.title: Kafka vs REST — which to use?
exercises.scenario: Different scenarios call for different communication patterns.
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, choose Kafka or REST and give one reason:

A. order-service needs current stock price before confirming a trade
B. A trade executes → SMS sent to user
C. A new user registers → welcome email sent
D. payment-service needs to verify a user account before charging
E. Order placed → 5 different services update their state
F. API Gateway routes an incoming request to the correct microservice
exercises.expected_solution:
A → REST (need price synchronously before trade can proceed)
B → Kafka (SMS is async — trade already confirmed, notification follows)
C → Kafka (welcome email doesn't block registration response)
D → REST (need verification before charging — can't proceed without it)
E → Kafka (one event, 5 listeners — perfect Kafka use case)
F → REST (real-time routing; synchronous by nature)

test_cases.expected:
A → REST (need price synchronously before trade can proceed)
B → Kafka (SMS is async — trade already confirmed, notification follows)
C → Kafka (welcome email doesn't block registration response)
D → REST (need verification before charging — can't proceed without it)
E → Kafka (one event, 5 listeners — perfect Kafka use case)
F → REST (real-time routing; synchronous by nature)

hints: REST: you need the response to proceed
Kafka: you don't need to wait; or multiple services need the same event

exercises.title: Kafka terminology — match the definition
exercises.scenario: Core Kafka vocabulary used in every interview and project.
exercises.instructions: Fill in the Kafka term for each definition.

blanks.hint: The 'subject' that producers write to and consumers read from
Topics are split into these for parallelism

hints: Topic → Partition → Offset: three levels of hierarchy
Producer writes, Consumer reads, Broker stores

exercises.title: Consumers vs partitions — how many are active?
exercises.scenario: A consumer group assigns one consumer per partition at most.
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, how many consumers are actively consuming? How many are idle?

A. Topic: 4 partitions. Consumer group: 2 consumers.
B. Topic: 4 partitions. Consumer group: 4 consumers.
C. Topic: 4 partitions. Consumer group: 6 consumers.
D. Topic: 1 partition. Consumer group: 3 consumers.
exercises.expected_solution:
A. 2 active (each handles 2 partitions), 0 idle
B. 4 active (each handles 1 partition), 0 idle — maximum parallelism
C. 4 active (each handles 1 partition), 2 idle (no partition to assign)
D. 1 active (handles the 1 partition), 2 idle

test_cases.expected:
A. 2 active (each handles 2 partitions), 0 idle
B. 4 active (each handles 1 partition), 0 idle — maximum parallelism
C. 4 active (each handles 1 partition), 2 idle (no partition to assign)
D. 1 active (handles the 1 partition), 2 idle

hints: Max active consumers = number of partitions
Extra consumers beyond partition count are idle standby

exercises.title: Design a Kafka topic for the Swiggy order system
exercises.scenario: Topic design is the foundational architecture decision.
exercises.instructions:
Design the Kafka topic structure for Swiggy:
1. List 3 topics with: name, partitions, retention, key strategy, reasoning
2. For each topic, list 2-3 consumer services
3. Which communication should remain as REST (not Kafka)?
hints: Group related event types in one topic (status changes = one topic)
Partition key = the entity you need ordering for
Audit/compliance topics: longer retention (months/years)

exercises.title: Docker Compose for local Kafka
exercises.scenario: Kafka runs locally via Docker for development.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete docker-compose.yml for a local Kafka setup:
1. Single broker in KRaft mode (no Zookeeper)
2. Port 9092 exposed
3. A CLUSTER_ID set
4. replication factor 1 (single node dev)
5. Show the CLI commands to: create a topic 'trade-events' with 3 partitions, produce a test message, consume from the beginning
exercises.expected_solution:
# docker-compose.yml:
version: '3'
services:
  kafka:
    image: confluentinc/cp-kafka:7.5.0
    hostname: kafka
    container_name: kafka
    ports:
      - '9092:9092'
    environment:
      KAFKA_NODE_ID: 1
      KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES: broker,controller
      KAFKA_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://0.0.0.0:9092,CONTROLLER://0.0.0.0:9093
      KAFKA_ADVERTISED_LISTENERS: PLAINTEXT://localhost:9092
      KAFKA_LISTENER_SECURITY_PROTOCOL_MAP: PLAINTEXT:PLAINTEXT,CONTROLLER:PLAINTEXT
      KAFKA_CONTROLLER_QUORUM_VOTERS: 1@kafka:9093
      KAFKA_CONTROLLER_LISTENER_NAMES: CONTROLLER
      KAFKA_OFFSETS_TOPIC_REPLICATION_FACTOR: 1
      KAFKA_DEFAULT_REPLICATION_FACTOR: 1
      KAFKA_MIN_INSYNC_REPLICAS: 1
      CLUSTER_ID: MkU3OEVBNTcwNTJENDM2Qk

# CLI commands:
# Start:
# docker compose up -d

# Create topic:
# docker exec kafka kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
#   --create --topic trade-events --partitions 3 --replication-factor 1

# Describe topic:
# docker exec kafka kafka-topics --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 \
#   --describe --topic trade-events

# Produce a message:
# docker exec -it kafka kafka-console-producer \
#   --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --topic trade-events
# > {"tradeId":"TRD-001","symbol":"TCS","qty":10}

# Consume from beginning:
# docker exec kafka kafka-console-consumer \
#   --bootstrap-server localhost:9092 --topic trade-events \
#   --from-beginning --group test-consumer-group

public class KafkaSetup {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Kafka: KRaft mode, port 9092, no Zookeeper");
    }
}
hints: KRaft mode: KAFKA_PROCESS_ROLES=broker,controller (no Zookeeper needed)
CLUSTER_ID: required for KRaft initialization
--from-beginning: read all messages from offset 0

exercises.title: Message ordering and partition keys
exercises.scenario: Same key = same partition = ordering preserved.
exercises.instructions:
For each scenario, what partition key should be used and why?

1. trade-events: preserving per-user trade order for portfolio updates
2. nse-tick-data: stock price updates needing per-symbol ordering
3. audit-log: all events, no ordering requirement between users
4. notification-events: SMS/email, no ordering needed

Show how key assignment affects partitioning with an example.
hints: Key = same key → same partition (hash(key) % numPartitions)
null key → round-robin (no ordering guarantee)
Choose key based on what needs ordering, not what's unique

exercises.title: Design Kafka architecture for Zerodha trading
exercises.scenario: Apply Kafka topic design to a complete trading platform.
exercises.instructions:
Design the full Kafka architecture for Zerodha:
1. Identify 4 Kafka topics (trade-events, market-ticks, notification-events, audit-log)
2. For each: partitions, key, retention, what events it carries
3. Draw (in text) the producer → topic → consumer groups flow
4. Identify what stays as REST (synchronous calls)
5. Show as a Java enum the event types for 'trade-events'
exercises.starter_code:
// Zerodha Kafka Architecture:
// 4 topics + enum + flow diagram
hints: Group related events (PLACED, EXECUTED, CANCELLED) in ONE topic with event type field
market-ticks: high volume, short retention; audit-log: low volume, long retention
REST = critical path (needs answer); Kafka = side effects (don't need answer)

exercises.title: Consumer group rebalancing — who reads what?
exercises.scenario: When a consumer joins or leaves, Kafka rebalances partition assignments.
exercises.instructions:
Topic 'order-events' has 4 partitions (P0, P1, P2, P3).
Consumer group 'notification-svc' starts with 2 consumers (C1, C2).

Describe partition assignments at each step:
1. Initial state with C1, C2
2. C3 joins
3. C4 joins
4. C5 joins
5. C2 crashes (rebalance after C2 leaves)
exercises.expected_solution:
1. Initial (C1, C2):
   C1: P0, P1
   C2: P2, P3

2. C3 joins (rebalance):
   C1: P0, P1
   C2: P2
   C3: P3

3. C4 joins (rebalance):
   C1: P0
   C2: P1
   C3: P2
   C4: P3
   (Maximum parallelism: 4 consumers, 4 partitions)

4. C5 joins (rebalance):
   C1: P0
   C2: P1
   C3: P2
   C4: P3
   C5: idle (no partition to assign)

5. C2 crashes (rebalance):
   C1: P0, P1
   C3: P2
   C4: P3
   C5: now active (takes P1 from C1 actually — depends on Kafka's assignment)
   More precisely: P1 is reassigned to C5 (the standby consumer)
   C1: P0, C3: P2, C4: P3, C5: P1
hints: Max active = num partitions. Extra consumers = standby.
Rebalance: all partitions redistributed when group membership changes
Crashed consumer: its partitions go to remaining consumers

exercises.title: Message retention — what can be replayed?
exercises.scenario: Kafka retains messages for a configured period — enabling replay.
exercises.instructions:
Write a diagram (in text/comments) and explanation for:
1. Topic 'trade-events' with 7-day retention
2. notification-svc (current offset: 5000) crashes on day 3
3. notification-svc restarts on day 5 — where does it resume?
4. New service 'tax-svc' starts on day 6 with '--from-beginning'
5. What happens to notification-svc messages from days 1-3 after day 10?

Include a Java class explaining offset management.
hints: Kafka stores committed offsets in __consumer_offsets topic
Restart: consumer resumes from last committed offset
--from-beginning: resets group offset to 0 (reads all retained messages)

exercises.title: Topic naming conventions and schemas
exercises.scenario: Consistent naming and schemas make Kafka maintainable at scale.
exercises.instructions:
Define naming conventions and event schema for a Razorpay-style payment Kafka system:
1. Topic naming: pattern for {domain}-{entity}-{action}s
2. Event schema as Java record: include eventId, eventType, timestamp, payload
3. Show 3 concrete topics with 2 example event types each
4. Explain why eventId is important for idempotency
hints: eventId: UUID allows consumers to detect and skip duplicates
traceId: links Kafka messages to distributed traces (M15)
Generic KafkaEvent<T>: reusable wrapper for all event types

exercises.title: Kafka topic planner — throughput calculation
exercises.scenario: Partition count is derived from throughput requirements.
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha has these requirements:
- trade-events: 50,000 trades/hour peak, each notification takes 10ms to process
- market-ticks: 5 million ticks/hour, each takes 2ms

Calculate:
1. How many notification-svc instances are needed for trade-events?
2. How many partitions are needed?
3. How many market-data-processor instances for market-ticks?
4. How many partitions for market-ticks?
5. Show the calculation as Java code
exercises.starter_code: // Throughput calculation for Kafka partition sizing
hints: Throughput per consumer = 1000ms / processingTimeMs * 3600s
Consumers needed = total throughput / throughput per consumer (ceiling)
Partitions >= consumers needed (one consumer per partition max)

exercises.title: Event-driven refactor — order service
exercises.scenario: Refactor synchronous calls to Kafka events where appropriate.
exercises.instructions:
Show the before (all synchronous) and after (Kafka for async parts) for `OrderService.placeOrder()`:

BEFORE: synchronous calls to 5 services (restaurant check, payment, notification, analytics, portfolio)
AFTER: synchronous for restaurant + payment; Kafka for notification, analytics, portfolio

Show the structure clearly with comments on what changed and why.
exercises.starter_code:
// BEFORE: all synchronous
// AFTER: hybrid — sync for critical path, Kafka for side effects
hints: SYNC: restaurant check (is it open?) + payment (must succeed first)
ASYNC: notification, analytics, portfolio — don't block the user
ONE Kafka event, MANY consumers — removes N synchronous calls

exercises.title: Complete Kafka architecture document
exercises.scenario: Design the full Kafka topology for a fintech system.
exercises.instructions:
Write a complete Kafka architecture document for PhonePe-style UPI payments:
1. 4 topics (upi.payment.events, upi.fraud.events, upi.notification.events, upi.audit.log)
2. Full event schema as Java records for each
3. Producer-topic-consumer mapping
4. Partition strategy and retention for each
5. What stays as REST (UPI requires sync approval)

All records must compile.
exercises.starter_code:
// PhonePe UPI Kafka Architecture
// Complete records + topology
exercises.expected_solution:
import java.time.Instant;
import java.util.*;

// ── EVENT RECORDS ─────────────────────────────────────────────────
record PaymentEvent(
    String eventId,            // UUID for idempotency
    String eventType,          // PAYMENT_INITIATED, AUTHORIZED, CAPTURED, FAILED
    String traceId,
    Instant timestamp,
    PaymentDetails payload
) {}
record PaymentDetails(String paymentId, String fromVpa, String toVpa,
                       double amount, String utrNumber) {}

record FraudEvent(
    String eventId, String paymentId,
    String riskLevel,          // LOW, MEDIUM, HIGH, BLOCKED
    List<String> signals,
    Instant detectedAt
) {}

record NotificationEvent(
    String eventId, String userId,
    String channel,            // SMS, WHATSAPP, PUSH
    String template, Map<String,String> params
) {}

record AuditEvent(
    String eventId, String entityId, String action,
    String actorService, String ipAddress,
    Instant timestamp, Map<String,Object> before, Map<String,Object> after
) {}

// ── KAFKA TOPOLOGY ────────────────────────────────────────────────
class UpiKafkaTopology {
    /*
    Topic: upi.payment.events
      Partitions: 12 (high throughput: ~1M payments/day)
      Key: paymentId (ordering per payment, e.g., INITIATED → AUTHORIZED → CAPTURED)
      Retention: 30 days
      Producer: payment-service
      Consumers:
        - fraud-detection-svc (group: fraud-detect) — checks signals
        - settlement-svc (group: settlement) — batches for bank settlement
        - analytics-svc (group: analytics) — dashboards

    Topic: upi.fraud.events
      Partitions: 6
      Key: userId
      Retention: 90 days
      Producer: fraud-detection-svc
      Consumers:
        - notification-svc (group: fraud-notify) — alert user on HIGH risk
        - risk-engine-svc (group: risk-engine) — update user risk profile
        - compliance-svc (group: compliance) — regulatory reporting

    Topic: upi.notification.events
      Partitions: 6
      Key: null (no ordering needed)
      Retention: 3 days
      Producer: payment-svc, fraud-svc
      Consumers:
        - sms-gateway (group: sms)
        - whatsapp-gateway (group: whatsapp)
        - push-gateway (group: push)

    Topic: upi.audit.log
      Partitions: 6
      Key: userId
      Retention: 7 years (RBI compliance)
      Producer: ALL services
      Consumers:
        - compliance-svc, audit-reporting-svc

    REST stays for:
      - UPI PIN verification (bank server sync call — mandatory RBI flow)
      - Account balance check before payment
      - Payment authorization (NPCI sync approval needed)
    */
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("4 topics: payment, fraud, notification, audit");
        System.out.println("REST: UPI PIN, balance check, NPCI authorization");
    }
}
hints: Payment events: high partition count for throughput; INITIATED→CAPTURED ordering per paymentId
Audit log: 7 years retention for financial compliance
REST for UPI: NPCI requires synchronous authorization before payment completes

exercises.title: Kafka vs RabbitMQ — when to choose what
exercises.scenario: Different message systems suit different use cases.
exercises.instructions:
Write a `MessageSystemAdvisor` class with a method `recommend(requirements)` that:
1. Takes: throughput (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW), ordering required (boolean), replay needed (boolean), multiple consumers (boolean)
2. Returns recommendation: KAFKA or RABBITMQ with justification
3. Hardcode recommendations for 4 scenarios: audit logs, notification queue, trade events, simple task queue
hints: Kafka: replay, multiple independent consumers, high throughput
RabbitMQ: simple task queue, single consumer, message TTL after ack
Both handle ordering within a queue/partition

exercises.title: Zerodha full Kafka topology — Topic 16.1 synthesis
exercises.scenario: Design and document the complete event-driven Zerodha platform.
exercises.instructions:
Create the complete Kafka topology for Zerodha's trading platform:
1. TradeEvent, MarketTickEvent, NotificationEvent, AuditEvent records (full fields)
2. 4 Kafka topics defined as constants with partition/retention metadata
3. Producer-consumer mapping as a documented class
4. Throughput calculation: trade-events at 100K trades/hour, 5ms processing
5. Hybrid architecture: sync (market quote, account verify) + async (everything else)
6. main() proves all records are instantiable

All must compile.
exercises.starter_code: // Complete Zerodha Kafka topology — Topic 16.1 synthesis
hints: 200 msg/s per consumer × 3600s = 720K per hour; 100K/720K = 1 consumer (with headroom → 6 partitions)
SYNC = critical path (can't proceed without answer); ASYNC = side effects
All 4 records must have eventId (UUID) for idempotency
